title.rendered: Postcards from Another Planet: Tatacoa Like You've Never Seen It

excerpt.rendered: A journey into the dry heart of Huila, where the earth dresses in red and grey, the silence sounds deep, and the sky becomes a spectacle every night. The Tatacoa Desert gave us landscapes that look like they belong to another planet, moments of pure connection with nature, and an experience you never forget. Through the lens of Ibai Fernández, these images capture the magic of that corner of the world. I hope that, as you look at them, you can feel the heat of the sun, the wind among the cacti, and the vastness of this arid paradise.

content.rendered:
A journey into the dry heart of Huila, where the earth dresses in red and grey, the silence sounds deep, and the sky becomes a spectacle every night. The Tatacoa Desert gave us landscapes that look like they belong to another planet, moments of pure connection with nature, and an experience you never forget.

Through the lens of Ibai Fernández, these images capture the magic of that corner of the world. I hope that, as you look at them, you can feel the heat of the sun, the wind among the cacti, and the vastness of this arid paradise.

Red Desert (El Cuzco)

El Cuzco is the red heart of the Tatacoa. A landscape sculpted by wind and time, where the earth rises like petrified waves. Walking through here is like stepping into a Martian dream: canyons, whimsical formations, and a silence that reverberates. The light at golden hour transforms everything into something otherworldly — reds that shift to amber, shadows that stretch like ancient stories across the cracked ground.

Grey Desert (Los Hoyos)

Twenty minutes from the red, the grey. Another face of the same desert. Here the earth is softer, as if it had been weeping for centuries. The formations are less aggressive, more melancholic. And in the middle of the dust, a natural pool: an unexpected oasis. This is the perfect spot for those who want to sit, breathe, and listen to the ancient murmur of the landscape.

The Sky from the Astronomical Observatory

When night falls, the Tatacoa lights up its most powerful show: the sky. Without city lights to dim it, the firmament unfolds in all its splendor. At one of the observatories, a passionate astronomer guided us among constellations and planets with the precision of a poet. Orion, the Southern Cross, Jupiter — names we had heard before, but here they became intimate. Lying on a blanket, with the dog asleep at our feet, we felt small and enormous at the same time.

Postcards

Some places resist description. They ask to be seen, felt, breathed. The Tatacoa Desert is one of those places. These photographs are not just images — they are fragments of a living memory, a reminder that the world holds corners that still manage to leave us speechless.

title.rendered: My Worst Airbnb Experience: A Warning for Digital Nomads and Travelers

excerpt.rendered: After more than two years of traveling and using Airbnb as our main accommodation platform, this has been without a doubt the worst experience we've had. I lived through it all during a month in a place that promised to be ideal — but turned out to be a disaster from day one. This post is not just a catharsis, but a real warning for those looking for peaceful, comfortable, and reliable stays, especially if, like us, you work remotely.

content.rendered:
My Worst Airbnb Experience: A Warning for Digital Nomads and Travelers

After more than two years of traveling and using Airbnb as our main accommodation platform, this has been without a doubt the worst experience we've had. I lived through it all during a month in a place that promised to be ideal, but turned out to be a disaster from day one.

This post is not just a catharsis — it's a real warning for those looking for peaceful, comfortable, and reliable stays, especially if, like us, you work remotely. Here I'll tell you why I would never book with this host again, and why you shouldn't either.

Misleading Information from the Very Start

We booked the listing because it clearly stated it was a full house with five bedrooms, two bathrooms, and pet- and smoker-friendly. However, when we arrived, we found ourselves in a small two-bedroom, one-bathroom apartment. We were three people and a dog (the third person was arriving a few days later), but seeing only two bedrooms, we asked what was going on. We were told there must have been an error and that they could offer us an extra room upstairs — sharing the bathroom with other guests.

We showed the listing to make clear we hadn't booked that. The person who received us was not the host and asked us to wait for her to arrive. When she finally did, we reviewed the listing together — and surprise — it no longer said five bedrooms, but three. In other words, they had modified the listing while we were already there.

The listing: when we booked

The listing: after we arrived at the house

Despite all this, due to personal circumstances we couldn't relocate at that moment, so we agreed to stay. Later, the host came back to tell us that the kitchen and laundry were actually shared with the upstairs rooms, and that if we smoked, we had to use the terrace. That was the last straw. We told her that was too much — we hadn't booked a shared space. She eventually came back, looking deflated, to say that yes, the apartment would be just for us.

Something we discovered after leaving was that the property is listed on other platforms like Booking.com with a photo of the house across the street. The actual house is an interior property, accessed through the black gate on the right.

An Absent and Indifferent Host

Throughout the entire month, communication was minimal or nonexistent. We had to write to her multiple times and wait more than 48 hours for a reply — if one came at all. But the most serious incident was when I got locked inside the house. The lock was broken and I couldn't open the door. I messaged asking for help and she never responded. If I had been alone in the country or without access to other people, I would have been trapped all day.

After so many good experiences with attentive, available hosts who are always ready to help, this indifference was truly bewildering.

Still waiting for her to help me 🥹👉🏻

Breakdowns and Deplorable Conditions

From day one, we had to deal with serious problems throughout the house. The bathroom and kitchen pipes were broken, so every time we brushed our teeth or washed the dishes, water would leak and we'd have to spend at least ten minutes mopping up the floor.

Cleanliness was nowhere to be found: everything was covered in dust. We had to thoroughly clean the wardrobes and kitchen cabinets before we could put any of our things away. The pillows were so worn out that we had neck pain for almost the entire stay.

And as if that weren't enough...

The Internet: A Nightmare for Digital Nomads

The internet was our biggest headache. It went down up to five times a day, and as remote workers, this directly affected our productivity and peace of mind. There is nothing more frustrating than having meetings cancelled, files that won't upload, and depending on luck to meet your professional commitments. In 2025, this is simply unacceptable — and even more so when the host refuses to take responsibility or propose a solution.

Am I Exaggerating?

I asked myself that at times. But as soon as we left that Airbnb and arrived at a new place in Santa Marta, everything changed: a host who is present, responds within minutes, takes an interest in our needs, helps us get around the city, and makes us feel genuinely welcome. That confirmed it — I wasn't asking for too much. I was simply expecting what Airbnb should guarantee.

Another thing that validated my complaint was the platform's own response. When I filed a formal complaint with Airbnb, attaching photos, videos, and screenshots as evidence of everything that had gone wrong, Airbnb reviewed the case and refunded us 30% of the total stay. That only happens when they consider the complaint legitimate and the listing did not deliver what was promised.

Conclusion

Neither the house nor the host is ready to receive guests through Airbnb. This experience taught us to research even more carefully before booking, to screenshot the full listing, and above all, to trust our first impressions. If something feels off from the start, it probably won't improve with time.

I hope this post helps someone else avoid a bad experience like ours. If you don't believe me, see what other travelers have to say.

Tips for Travelers and Digital Nomads

After this experience, I'd like to leave some recommendations for other travelers — and especially for those who, like me, work while moving from city to city:

  1. Read the listing description with a magnifying glass. Verify how many bedrooms, bathrooms, and shared areas are offered. If something sounds too good to be true, it probably is.
  2. Check the most recent reviews. Previous guests have often already noticed the same problems. Reviews are a goldmine of information that can help you decide.
  3. Contact the host before booking. Ask key questions: is the space fully private? Is there a reliable internet connection? Do they accept pets? The speed and clarity of their response will give you an idea of what communication will be like during your stay.
  4. Check the cancellation policy. If serious problems arise, it's important to know what protection you have if you decide to leave.
  5. Document everything from day one. Take photos, save screenshots, and keep records of all communication with the host. If something is wrong, having evidence will make any Airbnb claim much easier.
  6. Don't normalize bad service. As digital nomads, we depend on a basic quality of life to work well. Demanding what was promised is not asking for too much. Trust your instincts.

Traveling should be an enriching experience, not a constant struggle for decent conditions. I hope this story helps others avoid going through the same thing.

If you want more details about our dreadful experience, head over to Ibai's blog and read his review — it's quite entertaining.

title.rendered: 7 Things You Need to Know Before Visiting the Tatacoa Desert

excerpt.rendered: My personal experience exploring the Tatacoa Desert over three unforgettable days. More than a travel guide, this is a candid chat between friends — I'll tell you what I lived, what I learned, and what surprised me about this incredible place. So if you're thinking of visiting, or simply curious, here's my story, complete with sincere recommendations, excitement, and red dust on my shoes.

content.rendered:
My personal experience exploring the Tatacoa Desert over three unforgettable days. More than a travel guide, this is a candid chat between friends — I'll tell you what I lived, what I learned, and what surprised me about this incredible place. So if you're thinking of visiting, or simply curious, here's my story, complete with sincere recommendations, genuine excitement, and red dust in my shoes.

1. Planning the Perfect Getaway

It all started with a free Friday and a long-held wish: to see that corner of earth that looks like another planet. The Tatacoa Desert, in the south of Huila, was calling to us with its dry voice and promise of a starlit silence.

We drove from Bogotá with our dog Pirata, chasing freedom and roads with no rush. We rented a car from Alquicarros (+57 318 7703670) for COP $800,000 for three days. We went through roughly six tolls (COP $8,000 each) and spent COP $80,000 on fuel.

We booked accommodation a week ahead. After browsing Booking.com and social media, we settled on Tatacoa Star Eco Hotel (+57 310 2557207), a pet-friendly place where the desert seemed to embrace you from the window. We paid COP $200,000 per night for a triple room, breakfast and parking included.

Our guide appeared along the way, as unexpected things do. Robinson Cleves [+57 311 8380407] led us through the hidden trails of the desert, sharing stories of wind, fossils, and solitude for COP $120,000.

Eating in the Tatacoa has its own poetry: simple, delicious dishes that taste even better after a long walk. Each meal cost us around COP $30,000.

2. Places You Cannot Miss

🏜️ Red Desert (El Cuzco)

This is the red heart of the Tatacoa. A landscape sculpted by wind and time, where the earth rises like petrified waves. Walking here is like stepping into a Martian dream: deep ravines, impossible shapes, earth that breathes memory.

The guide turns this hike into a journey through time. He showed us "the turtle", "the friar", and other figures carved by the randomness of climate. Everything here is heat, light, and dust that gets into your soul. Bring water, a hat, and clothes that aren't afraid of the sun.

🏞️ Grey Desert (Los Hoyos)

Twenty minutes from the red, the grey. Another face of the same desert. Here the earth is softer, as if it had wept for centuries. The formations are less aggressive, more melancholic. And in the middle of the dust, a natural pool: an unexpected oasis.

We learned that the Tatacoa is not actually a desert, but a tropical dry forest. That where there are cracks today, millions of years ago there was sea. And that the Candelabro cacti store wood inside them, as if they want to remember their kinship with trees.

This spot is perfect for those who want to sit, breathe, and listen to the ancient murmur of the landscape.

🔭 Astronomical Observatory

When night falls, the Tatacoa puts on its most powerful show: the sky. Without city lights to dull it, the firmament unfolds in all its glory. We visited one of the observatories, where a passionate astronomer guided us among constellations and planets with the precision of a poet.

Orion, the Southern Cross, Jupiter... names we knew but that here became intimate. Lying on a blanket, with the dog asleep at our feet, we felt small and enormous at the same time. An experience you can't fully explain — you can only live it.

3. Things We Didn't Get to Do

  • Mirador de Ventanas: A natural balcony for watching sunrises and sunsets. We saved it for another visit.
  • Night photography session: Night photo shoots in the desert are very popular, but we were more interested in visiting the observatory.

4. Things We Wouldn't Do Again

  • Leaving Bogotá at 5:00 p.m.: Traffic trapped us for two hours. Next time, we'll leave at sunrise.
  • Driving at night between Neiva and Villavieja: The darkness makes the road feel mysterious, but also uncomfortable. Better to do that stretch during the day, when the scenery accompanies you.

5. Key Recommendations

  • Light clothing, a hat, sunglasses, sunscreen, and water — these will be your best allies.
  • The entire desert is pet-friendly, but take care of your animals: there are many goats in the area that may tempt their instinct to chase.
  • The dunes must be preserved — do not walk on them.
  • Wear comfortable shoes, because you'll be doing a lot of walking.
  • For the observatory: bring a blanket, a light jacket, and a willingness to look up at the sky without any rush.

6. Estimated Travel Costs

  • Car rental: COP $800,000 (3 days)
  • Tolls: COP $48,000 (approx. 6 tolls)
  • Fuel: COP $80,000
  • Accommodation: COP $200,000 per night (triple room)
  • Tour guide: COP $120,000
  • Meals: COP $30,000 average per dish

7. Useful Information

Location: Tatacoa Desert, municipality of Villavieja, department of Huila, Colombia.

Travel time from Bogotá: 7–8 hours by road.

Nearest airport: Benito Salas Airport, in Neiva.

Flights to Neiva: There are flights from Bogotá with airlines such as Avianca, Satena, and EasyFly. Prices typically range between COP $200,000 and $400,000 depending on the season.

Bus from Bogotá: You can take a bus from Bogotá's main bus terminal to Neiva with companies like Coomotor, Bolivariano, or Cootranshuila. The journey takes 5–6 hours and costs between COP $60,000 and $80,000 depending on the season. From Neiva you can take another bus to Villavieja (approx. COP $10,000) or hire a mototaxi (COP $15,000–$20,000).

If you're looking for an out-of-the-ordinary destination with otherworldly landscapes, star-filled skies, and a very special energy, the Tatacoa Desert is the perfect place. Don't miss it!

title.rendered: Where Silence Speaks: A Chronicle of a Journey to the Tatacoa Desert

excerpt.rendered: On a cold Bogotá afternoon, with dusk whispering in our ears, we took the NQS heading south. The grey sky bid us farewell as the city tried to hold us back in its embrace of concrete and traffic. We were heading to Neiva — but in truth, our adventure had begun much earlier, the moment a free Friday became a shared destination.

content.rendered:
On a cold Bogotá afternoon, with dusk whispering in our ears, we took the NQS heading south. The grey sky bid us farewell as the city tried to hold us back in its embrace of concrete and traffic. We were heading to Neiva — but in truth, our adventure had begun much earlier, the moment a free Friday became a shared destination.

It was a lucky coincidence: Ibai and I discovered we had the same day free to escape. And without thinking too hard about it, we knew exactly where we wanted to go. The Tatacoa Desert — that name that sounds like wind, red dust, and a starry sky — was calling us.

We considered flights, guided tours, easy routes. But we decided to write our own itinerary. We wanted freedom. Improvisation. And Pirata, our faithful and stubborn dog, didn't exactly have the soul of an air passenger. So we rented a car and hit the road.

An adventure full of delicious food, kind people, unique landscapes, learning, and connection with nature. The Tatacoa Desert stayed in our hearts... and on our palates. — Món Mont

The Journey There

We left on a Thursday at sundown, just as we closed our laptops. It was five o'clock sharp when we set off, but Bogotá, as always, wouldn't let us go without a fight. Traffic made one last attempt to hold us back. Two hours later, the city was finally behind us.

The road stretched out like a dark ribbon through the mountains. We descended southward, passing through sleeping villages, endless curves, and skies that slowly turned to ink. Insects hit the windshield like misguided shooting stars. At midnight we arrived in Neiva, and the adventure was far from over.

One last stretch remained: Villavieja. A narrow, silent road lined with trees that seemed like guardians of legends. We drove in silence, eyes wide open and hearts decided.

At one in the morning — Pirata slightly queasy and both of us exhausted — we arrived at the hotel. Jaiver, our host, opened the door with a smile that soothed the soul. In that moment, all we wanted was a bed and a little peace.

The First Full Day

Friday dawned clear, with promises of open sky. I worked half the day from the hotel terrace while Ibai and his father went out to explore. Being a digital nomad has its contrasts: Zoom calls with cacti in the background, and keyboard sounds keeping rhythm with faraway birds.

At midday, we had lunch at "La Casa del Cabrito" (The Goat House). We tried the stewed goat, the sancocho, and every bite felt like a celebration of the place itself. Giant portions, honest flavor. We ordered extra to take away and had it for breakfast the next morning. We discovered new sweets: goat's milk fudge that melted in your mouth, the classic matrimonio (sweet and salty cheese), and an unexpected, delicious cactus candy.

In the afternoon, we wandered without hurry. We had coffee at Café Mael, surrounded by flowers and easy conversation. As evening fell, the town offered us ice cream and a warm breeze. Back at the hotel, Jaiver surprised us again — part receptionist, part folk dancer, part storyteller. Listening to him was like opening a living book, a masterclass in history and heart. Two hours slipped by without us noticing.

The Desert Day

Saturday was the big day. Robinson, our guide, arrived on time with a wise man's smile and a hiker's stride. He took us first to El Cuzco, the red desert. There the landscape seemed to belong to another planet: deep grooves, impossible shapes, earth that breathed memory.

Then Los Hoyos, the grey desert. Softer, more serene. There, a natural pool gave us back our breath under the relentless sun. We walked among the cacti, learned about fossils, and about the paradox of the place: the Tatacoa, which is not really a desert at all, but a dry forest that was once a sea.

And as a crowning moment, the night wrapped us in magic: the astronomical observatory. We lay on the grass while an astronomer-poet guided us across the sky with his beam of light. We saw constellations with mythological names — Orion, Cassiopeia, Ursa Major, the Southern Cross. Even Jupiter winked at us.

The Road Home

On Sunday, the return journey showed us its other face. The road that had seemed so mysterious in the dark revealed itself as picturesque under the sun. We learned a simple truth: darkness magnifies fear, but light always reveals the beauty.

Those three days tasted like freedom. Like good food, radiant people, and living earth. The Tatacoa Desert stayed with us — like dust in our shoes and stars in our memory.

title.rendered: 9 Steps to Get Started as a Digital Nomad

excerpt.rendered: Keep this in mind before you start your adventure as a digital nomad. Getting started might seem overwhelming, but following a few basic steps makes the process much more manageable — here are mine, along with my personal experience at each stage.

content.rendered:
Keep this in mind before you start your adventure as a digital nomad.

Getting started as a digital nomad can feel like an overwhelming task, but if you follow a few essential first steps, the process becomes much more manageable. It's worth remembering that these steps are just an initial guide and may vary depending on each person's needs and priorities.

Below, I'll share the key steps to begin this adventure, along with my personal experience at each stage, to help you prepare your own path toward a life of digital nomadism.

Step 1: Choose Your Path

Determine your approach as a digital nomad. Will you offer freelance services, build an online business, look for remote employment, or become a content creator? Define your area of interest and your strengths. This initial decision will influence the kind of skills you'll need to develop and the opportunities you'll pursue.

The first thing you should know is that you don't have to choose just one path. If you're a multitasker, you can do several things — but be careful not to overload yourself with work, because then what's the point of choosing this nomadic life if you can't enjoy the place you're in?

In my case, this was one of the first decisions I made. I knew that working as a freelancer with my own marketing agency, which was already generating good income, was a viable option — but I also needed a stable monthly income for peace of mind. So I set out to find remote employment as well.

Today I work for my freelance clients through my agency, for a marketing agency in Chile, and I create content for my blog and social media — though that last part happens when my work commitments and excursion schedules allow it, since it's not part of my income and I don't let it take up more time than necessary.

Step 2: Build Your Skills

Develop the skills needed for your chosen path. This could include technical skills, communication, digital marketing, or content production. Enrolling in online courses, attending workshops, and participating in professional communities will help you improve your abilities and stay current with industry trends.

I made the decision to live as a digital nomad back in 2018, and from that point I worked on my skills. Along the way I took many courses to sharpen my graphic design, web design, and multimedia design abilities. Then I completed a master's degree in Digital Marketing and E-commerce. Currently I'm doing further training to specialize in UX/UI. Staying up to date is essential — it makes remote work opportunities and finding clients much more accessible, because you're current in your field and can offer a better service or product.

Step 3: Establish Your Online Presence

Build a strong online presence through a website, social media profiles, and relevant platforms for your field. This will help you connect with clients, audiences, or collaborators. A well-presented portfolio and testimonials from satisfied clients can boost your credibility and attract new opportunities.

Even if you don't intend to monetize your digital channels, it's important to build a solid presence — because when you're constantly on the move, this becomes your calling card, your proof that you are indeed a digital nomad. And don't just create them — keep them as up to date as possible, because when you arrive somewhere new, this can help you establish trust with the services you need to contract.

It also serves as a presentation to clients or companies you want to apply to for remote work. I have my personal channels where I share my experiences as a digital nomad and my explorations of the places I visit, as well as my Marketing Agency's channels where I share all the information related to my work.

Step 4: Plan Your Finances

Make sure you have a solid financial plan. Consider travel costs, accommodation, health insurance, and other expenses. Maintain an emergency fund and set appropriate prices for your services. Use financial management tools to track your income and expenses, and adjust your budget as needed.

Having a general plan at the start is always helpful, but in my experience, finances are something you need to review month by month — because when you're on the move, things change from place to place, even within the same country.

For example, rent varies from city to city. If you planned to spend $500 a month on accommodation with certain comforts, you might find that in another city you can get that price but without the comforts you planned for — or the opposite, that in a new city you can find something with more comforts for much less.

In my experience, the best approach I've found is to review my finances month by month and adapt according to where I am — both in terms of numbers and priorities. You need to learn to be flexible about certain things, and also be clear about your non-negotiables.

For me, in terms of housing, my non-negotiables are a private bathroom, a comfortable workspace, and high-speed internet — everything else I adapt to. Other non-negotiables are the experiences, excursions, and what the place has to offer for discovery, because without that part, the digital nomad life makes no sense to me.

Step 5: Choose Your Destinations

Research and select destinations that suit your work style and personal preferences. Consider factors like internet connection quality, cost of living, and safety. Joining digital nomad communities can offer valuable tips and recommendations about the best places to live and work.

This is one of the most exciting parts at the beginning, but stay grounded — don't let yourself be swept away only by emotion. Be practical when choosing your first destinations, because as you're just starting out, you don't know what to expect, and your first destinations will be the foundation of your learning about this lifestyle. So focus on researching things like costs and ease of access for flights, accommodation, food, and above all, whether the infrastructure exists to work remotely.

Following this approach, I decided my first country would be Colombia. It's close to my native Ecuador, which meant the flight wouldn't be too expensive, and it gave me a sense of security — even though I was confident in my decision, I didn't know what to expect, and having my country nearby was reassuring. Colombia also has ideal infrastructure for digital nomads, with a wide range of accommodation options designed specifically for us.

Step 6: Set Up Your Workspace

Create a functional workspace in your temporary locations. Make sure you have the tools and equipment needed to work efficiently. A well-organized work environment can improve your focus and productivity, allowing you to complete tasks effectively.

The first questions I ask when contacting a place to rent are: Is there a desk in the room? Is there a shared co-working space? Is there high-speed internet?

Yes — two workspaces and internet, because working remotely from my room every single day can feel a bit suffocating. So I like having the option to go to another space to work, and of course, the internet is non-negotiable.

Establish what your basic and priority needs are, and make sure the spaces you book can meet them. Of course, it's also up to you to make those spaces your own — a little aesthetic touch or a comfort item that makes you feel good and at ease while you work.

Step 7: Maintain Work-Life Balance

Maintain a healthy balance between work and exploration. Set schedules and boundaries to avoid burnout. Dedicate time to enjoy your travels and personal activities, making sure work doesn't interfere with your wellbeing and overall enjoyment.

Step 8: Build Your Network

Meet other digital nomads, locals, and professionals in your industry. This will give you not only collaboration opportunities, but also a sense of community. Participating in events, conferences, and online groups can help you build valuable relationships and find support along the way.

Step 9: Embrace Continuous Learning

The journey as a digital nomad is a process of constant learning. Take advantage of the opportunity to immerse yourself in new cultures, languages, and experiences. Stay open to new ideas and perspectives, and look for opportunities to continuously improve your skills and knowledge.

Final Reflection

Digital nomadism, although well defined today, depends greatly on what each person decides to prioritize and on the nature of their work. The beauty of this lifestyle lies in its versatility and its ability to adapt to a wide range of skills and professions. You can even innovate and become a digital nomad who does something unconventional, or who lives differently — because everyone seeks what best fits their own needs. Nothing is set in stone in the world of digital nomads. Each person has the freedom to design their own path, find their niche, and discover a way of life that is not only sustainable, but also enriching and deeply fulfilling.

title.rendered: Digital Nomads: Travel, Work, and Live on Your Own Terms

excerpt.rendered: Explore the world of the digital nomad and discover the different types and steps to get started. In an interconnected and technologically advanced world, the concept of the digital nomad has earned a prominent place — and it may be exactly the life you've been looking for.

content.rendered:
Explore the world of the digital nomad and discover the different types and steps to get started.

In an interconnected, technologically advanced world, the concept of the digital nomad has earned a prominent place. Being a digital nomad means breaking free from traditional geographic limitations and leveraging technology to work and live anywhere in the world. This constantly mobile lifestyle offers the chance to explore new cultures, connect with diverse people, and find a balance between work and personal life.

Let me invite you to discover the main characteristics of a nomadic life:

Freedom from Geographic Limitations

Being a digital nomad means shedding the traditional ties of a fixed workplace. Instead of being confined to an office or a specific location, you have the ability to work from anywhere with a reliable internet connection. This gives you the flexibility to travel, live in different cities or countries, and change your environment according to your preferences and needs. Imagine waking up on a paradisiacal beach in Thailand one month and in a vibrant European city the next — all while keeping your professional career going.

Technology as an Enabler

Technology is the backbone of the digital nomad lifestyle. Tools like laptops, smartphones, online collaboration apps, and communication platforms allow work to be done efficiently and effectively regardless of physical location. Thanks to software like Zoom, Slack, and Google Drive, you can maintain productivity and communication with clients, collaborators, and colleagues without issues. Automation and artificial intelligence are further easing the nomadic life, allowing people to delegate repetitive tasks and focus on creative and strategic work.

Exploration and Adventure

One of the most appealing aspects of being a digital nomad is the opportunity to explore the world. You can immerse yourself in different cultures, languages, landscapes, and traditions. Each new location brings the possibility of experiencing something new, enriching your perspective and broadening your horizon. From savoring local cuisine to participating in traditional festivals, every experience becomes a source of inspiration and learning that can positively influence your work and personal life.

Flexibility at Work

As a digital nomad, you have the ability to set your own schedules and routines. You can adapt your working day to your natural rhythm of productivity and to your personal commitments. This flexibility allows you to find a balance between your professional and personal life, which often results in greater overall wellbeing. You can choose to work intensively for a few days and then take a break to explore your surroundings, or integrate small pauses throughout the day to enjoy the sights and sounds of your temporary location.

Personal and Professional Growth

The digital nomad lifestyle fosters personal and professional development. Adapting to new environments and situations helps you cultivate skills in adaptability, problem-solving, and cross-cultural communication. And by connecting with people from different backgrounds, you expand your network and professional opportunities. Participating in local communities and attending professional events in different parts of the world can open unexpected doors and offer fresh, valuable perspectives for your career.

Challenges and Considerations

Although being a digital nomad comes with many advantages, it also carries challenges. These can include managing your workflow while traveling, adjusting to different time zones, finding reliable internet connections, and the need to maintain personal structure to avoid work fatigue. There may also be moments of loneliness and difficulties maintaining stable personal relationships due to constant mobility.

Types of Digital Nomads

The digital nomad lifestyle has evolved to encompass a wide variety of professions and activities. Each nomad finds their own path, adapting their skills and passions to a life of geographic freedom. From freelancers to content creators, digital nomads demonstrate that it is possible to work from anywhere in the world while exploring new cultures and living unique experiences. Here are the different types:

Global Freelancers

These digital nomads are independent professionals who offer their services online. Whether it's graphic design, writing, programming, or marketing, they work for clients around the world while traveling. Their ability to manage their time and location is essential for maintaining a balance between projects and exploration. The key for them is delivering high-quality results consistently, regardless of location.

Itinerant Entrepreneurs

These are individuals who have created their own online businesses. They might sell physical or digital products, offer online courses, or provide specialized services. The flexibility this approach offers allows for greater adaptability to market changes and the ability to run a business from any corner of the planet. Their success lies in their ability to innovate and quickly adapt to global market needs.

Remote Corporate Workers

Many companies have adopted remote work culture, allowing employees to work from anywhere. These digital nomads hold traditional corporate jobs but have the freedom to do them from any location. This gives them the chance to combine their work duties with travel and exploration. For them, a strong work ethic and self-management skills are crucial for meeting corporate expectations while enjoying their mobility.

Influencers and Content Creators

Influencers and content creators travel to capture unique experiences and share them online. Through blogs, vlogs, social media, and other channels, they share their adventures, knowledge, and cultural perspectives with their audience. They monetize their platforms through sponsorships and collaborations. Their life is a delicate balance between constantly creating engaging content and managing relationships with followers and business partners.

Educational Nomads

These individuals use technology to receive and provide online education. They may be students taking virtual courses while traveling, or educators teaching online classes from different parts of the world. Online education allows them to access global educational resources and contribute their knowledge to a diverse, global audience.

Customer Service Nomads

Some companies hire digital nomads to provide customer service and technical support from remote locations. This allows them to resolve queries and problems while enjoying geographic flexibility. The key for them is maintaining high levels of service and customer satisfaction, regardless of time zone or cultural differences.

Accounting and Finance Nomads

Professionals in accounting, finance, and consulting can carry out their work remotely, allowing them to work with clients from different regions while exploring new places. Precision and reliability are essential in their work, requiring meticulous organization and a disciplined approach.

Health and Wellness Nomads

Health experts — such as personal trainers, therapists, and nutritionists — can advise their clients online and offer virtual wellness programs, maintaining a global presence while helping others improve their quality of life. They use digital platforms to provide ongoing monitoring and support, adapting to each client's individual needs.

Tech Nomads

Software engineers, web developers, and other technology professionals can work remotely on digital projects. This gives them the opportunity to participate in innovative global projects. Their work often involves collaborating virtually as part of a team, using project management and online communication tools to coordinate efforts and meet deadlines.

Design and Creative Nomads

Graphic designers, visual artists, and creatives in general can offer their services online from anywhere. They can work on design, illustration, and visual content projects. The inspiration they draw from their ever-changing environment often reflects in their work, offering fresh and unique perspectives to their clients.

Translation and Language Nomads

People with skills in multiple languages can work as translators and offer interpretation services online. This allows them to collaborate with clients and companies from different parts of the world. Precision and cultural understanding are vital in their work, requiring deep linguistic and cultural knowledge.

Digital Marketing Nomads

Digital marketing experts — from strategists to social media specialists — can manage online campaigns for international clients while exploring new cultures. Their ability to understand and adapt to different global markets allows them to create effective and resonant strategies.

Final Reflection

Digital nomadism, although well defined today, depends greatly on what each person decides to prioritize and on the nature of their work. The beauty of this lifestyle lies in its versatility and its ability to adapt to a wide range of skills and professions. You can even innovate and become a digital nomad who does something unconventional, or who lives differently — because everyone seeks what best fits their own needs. Nothing is set in stone in the world of digital nomads. Each individual has the freedom to design their own path, find their niche, and discover a way of life that is not only sustainable, but enriching and fulfilling.

title.rendered: Childhood Dreams: The Story of a Digital Nomad

excerpt.rendered: On the anniversary of my first year as a digital nomad, I want to share my story — from a little girl dreaming of backpacking the world in a neighborhood in Guayaquil, Ecuador, to living and working from wherever I choose, one adventure at a time.

content.rendered:
On the anniversary of my first year as a digital nomad, I want to share my story.

Once upon a time, in a neighborhood in Guayaquil, Ecuador, there was a little girl who dreamed of being a backpacker. She watched those Anglo-Saxon travelers, backpacks on their shoulders, passing through her city, and couldn't stop thinking about the day she too would roam the world.

But life gave me a reality check. My mother wasn't on board, and although backpacking was cheap, I still needed money I didn't have. So I devised a plan: I would study tourism, and my career would take me around the world.

Another reality check arrived on my first day of university. One of my professors said: "You will spend four years getting to know Ecuador, every corner of it, learning about its traditions and culture. When you graduate, you will be the welcoming face for foreign tourists." That comment shattered my plan — but, ever the optimist, I found the silver lining: I had four years to travel my own country. How could I claim to explore the world without first knowing my own land?

More than four years passed, and I traveled and got to know my country deeply. I climbed mountains while exploring the highlands, discovering ancient traditions of the oldest indigenous communities. I navigated mighty rivers in the jungle, lived alongside indigenous tribes, and explored vast virgin rainforests. I marveled at the paradise beaches of the Pacific, connected with coastal communities, and savored the most delicious dishes from Ecuador's coast.

And so the years passed, in a "normal life". Jobs, relationships, friends, and projects came and went — but my biggest dream was always there, tucked away deep inside my mind and heart, where no one could see it. The few times I had dared to voice it, I'd been met with criticism or negativity about how it would never happen.

I grew up and grew wiser. Even I told myself that dream was impossible, that there was no way it could come true. But, as always, life made sure to show me that not everything has a mapped-out path — that possibilities are always there, and that I shouldn't give up on my dreams. You never know when, without realizing it, you start walking toward them.

How It All Began

In 2014, a year full of change, I was reborn after a long and difficult relationship. I was at my best — happy, doing what I loved, about to celebrate ten years as an event producer, and ready to explore new things in my personal life. In that search, I made new friends, and one of them introduced me to who is today my best friend.

When we first met, it seemed almost unreal that despite being very different, we shared several dreams. That connection meant that over ten years, we forged a relationship that went far beyond the traditional and the mundane.

But what does all this have to do with my first year as a digital nomad? Everything. Thanks to my childhood dream and this new person who came into my life, I was finally able to fulfill it.

So in 2014, my path toward becoming a digital nomad began — picking up that childhood dream again. First I changed cities, and although I loved my work, I made a radical career shift. I fell in love with graphic design, then web design, and finally multimedia design. I committed to learning everything I needed, and by 2016 I had landed my first job as a designer. Within two years, I had gone from designer to creative director. I gained so much experience and so many skills that by 2019, I was already working independently from home.

At that point, together with my adventure companion, we set our sights on a life of travel and began mentally preparing for that new life. But in 2020, we all know what happened: the pandemic. With all the lessons life had already taught me, I accepted the situation without giving up — with a vision for the future.

And so it was that in March 2023, we bought our first tickets from Ecuador to Colombia. We would start close to home, somewhere we already knew from vacations.

After a four-month process full of emotions of every kind — because you've surely seen plenty of videos where travelers say that famous line: "We sold everything and left to travel the world." It sounds simple, but the process is far longer, harder, and more painful than it appears. Still, one thing was certain: nothing was going to stop us.

Arriving in Colombia

We arrived in Colombia on June 17, 2023, at nine in the evening — a Spaniard, an Ecuadorian, a dog named Pirata, and six suitcases full of hope.

Bogotá welcomed us with open arms. That first morning, sunlight filtered through the curtains of the small apartment we had rented in Las Nieves, right in the heart of the city. From there, we began our adventure through the capital. One of the first things we did was climb the Cerro de Monserrate, where the panoramic view of the city took our breath away and reminded us of the vastness of the world we had decided to explore.

And so each day — between work, clients, and immigration paperwork — there was always the perfect moment to get to know and discover this city that welcomed us so warmly.

In La Candelaria, every alley was a journey through time. Colonial houses painted in vivid colors, street artists, and small cafés brought the neighborhood to life. The Botero Museum offered a unique vision of Colombian art, with Botero's voluminous figures inviting us to see beauty in the unusual.

One Sunday at the Jardín Botánico José Celestino Mutis. Among orchids and palms, we felt like we were in a small tropical paradise — a pause from the bustling city. Plaza Bolívar, with its imposing cathedral and the statue of the Liberator, reminded us of Colombia's rich history and its struggle for freedom.

Every day, the Seventh Avenue pedestrian walkway offered a new surprise. Music, dance, and the unmistakable energy of Bogotá filled us with joy. We tried traditional Colombian food: arepas (corn cakes), empanadas, ajiaco (a hearty potato and chicken soup), and bandeja paisa (a feast of a platter). But we also delighted in the city's international offerings, discovering new flavors at each restaurant we visited.

One of our most delicious experiences was visiting Paloquemao Market. There, amid the bustle of vendors and the aroma of fresh fruit, we discovered the diversity of Colombian cuisine. We tried exotic fruit juices, savored traditional tamales, and found the best Colombian arepas. And I couldn't resist buying a bouquet of tropical flowers to brighten up our apartment.

At La Perseverancia Market, we immersed ourselves in a feast of flavors. We tried fritanga (a mix of fried foods), ajiaco, and lechona (slow-roasted stuffed pork), each bite full of history and tradition. The vendors, always friendly, shared their stories and recipes with us, making every visit a lesson in culture and gastronomy.

Chía, with its famous Andrés Carne de Res restaurant, was a surreal experience. Among neon lights and live music, we enjoyed a night that felt like it came from a dream. The food, the drinks, the people — that evening became one of the most memorable of our time in Colombia.

The next stop was Villa de Leyva, a place where time seemed to have stood still. Cobblestone streets, colonial architecture, and an immense main square offered a respite from the fast pace of modern life. We immersed ourselves in the tranquility of the town, exploring every corner, marveling at the history and culture emanating from its walls.

Medellín: The City of Eternal Spring

Now I've arrived in Medellín, the City of Eternal Spring. Here, life has taken on a new rhythm. Its perfect climate has made me feel at home. I've let myself be swept along by the city's vibrant cultural life — from the Museum of Antioquia to Parque Explora. Every day I discover something new: a café serving the best tinto (black coffee) I've ever had, a conversation with a stranger who becomes a friend, a sunset from Cerro Nutibara that took my breath away.

The Plaza Minorista market has not disappointed — as always, a market is the best option. This one impressed me with its abundance of exotic fruits, the vendors with their stories, and the unique flavors of street food that have, as always, won my heart.

I've tried chorizo antioqueño (the famous Antioquian sausage), mondongo (tripe soup), and the delicious desserts of natilla (a cornstarch custard) and buñuelos (fried cheese fritters). All I can think is that I'd love to live closer to the market so I could visit it every day.

One Year of Living the Dream

After a year full of work, travel, discoveries, and living my dream, I feel like I've barely begun. The world is vast and full of wonders to explore. I'm excited about the new places I'll visit, the cultures I'll encounter, and the experiences I'll live.

The digital nomad life has taught me that there are no limits when it comes to pursuing dreams. Every place I visit, every person I meet, every story I live is a reminder that life is an adventure worth living to the fullest. And so, with a heart full of gratitude and a mind open to new possibilities, I continue my journey — knowing that the best is yet to come.

title.rendered: Street Food in Bogotá: A Feast at the Traditional Markets

excerpt.rendered: The essence of a city is reflected in its street food, and Bogotá is no exception. The city's traditional markets offer a unique culinary experience, where tempting aromas and authentic flavors intertwine with the everyday lives of bogotanos. Join me on a gastronomic journey through some of Bogotá's most iconic markets, where street food comes alive and takes your senses on a truly captivating trip.

content.rendered:
The essence of a city is reflected in its street food, and Bogotá is no exception. The city's traditional markets offer a unique culinary experience, where tempting aromas and authentic flavors intertwine with the everyday lives of bogotanos.

Join me on a gastronomic journey through some of Bogotá's most iconic markets, where street food comes alive and takes your senses on a truly captivating trip.

Street Food at La Perseverancia Market

Located in the heart of Bogotá's historic center, La Perseverancia market is a melting pot of colors, aromas, and flavors. Here, among stalls of fresh fruits and exotic flowers, you'll find a wide variety of gastronomic delights from the different regions of Colombia.

You can enjoy a delicious ajiaco santafereño (the classic Bogotá-style potato and chicken soup), the famous bandeja paisa (the iconic Antioquian feast platter), posta cartagenera (a slow-braised beef from Cartagena), cocido boyacense (a hearty stew from Boyacá), or a puchero santafereño (a traditional Bogotá broth).

Variety reigns supreme at La Perseverancia — you'll also find ceviches, frijolada (bean stew), fried fish, and catfish in coconut sauce. With so many delights, you'll leave satisfied and, above all, even more in love with Colombian food (if you already were).

And if you're like me — always needing a sweet finish — you'll find that here too: fruit salads, strawberries with cream, and the characteristic merengón (a big meringue dessert).

Address: Carrera 5 # 30A-30, Bogotá, Colombia

Street Food at Paloquemao Market

Known as the epicenter of food distribution in Bogotá, Paloquemao market is a must-stop for lovers of street food. Here, the freshness and quality of the products combine with culinary creativity to offer an unparalleled gastronomic experience.

To discover all that Paloquemao has to offer, you'll need to walk the entire market, because the food stalls aren't concentrated in one area — they're scattered throughout. But I promise you, as you wander through its (packed) corridors, you'll have a wonderful time discovering a colorful market full of incredibly friendly people.

At Paloquemao you'll find traditional Colombian dishes like lechona (slow-roasted stuffed pork), changua (a milky breakfast soup), arepas (corn cakes) of every variety, pandebono (cheese bread), empanadas, and yuca pastry. And of course, you can wash it all down with a traditional Colombian coffee.

And as if that weren't enough, you'll also find international dishes like tacos and sushi. A culinary visit to Paloquemao is an experience in itself!

Address: Av. Ciudad de Lima #25-04, Bogotá

Street Food at La Concordia Market

In the heart of the La Candelaria neighborhood, La Concordia market is a hidden gem that delights visitors with its authenticity and charm. Here, among its narrow corridors, you'll find fewer food stalls — but in their place, a wonderful variety of dishes that will win over your stomach... and your heart!

The wide range of traditional plates at La Concordia reflects the culinary richness of Colombia. You can enjoy cocido boyacense (Boyacá stew), fritanga (a mix of fried meats and sides), the popular sancocho de costilla (beef rib broth), frijolada (bean stew), and the best and most artisanal chorizos you'll find anywhere in Bogotá — plus, of course, the delicious ajiaco, flavorful fried mojarra fish, or mouthwatering ceviches.

To start, you can order traditional patacones (fried green plantain) or a ripe plantain with cheese and bocadillo (what we call membrillo — quince paste — in my beloved Ecuador). And to close your gastronomic visit, La Concordia offers a sweet and refreshing salpicón (a fruit punch dessert).

Address: Calle 12C # 1-40, Bogotá

Conclusion

Bogotá's traditional markets are much more than simple shopping destinations — they are authentic gastronomic paradises where local flavors blend with everyday life. La Perseverancia, Paloquemao, and La Concordia are just three of the many markets in Bogotá that offer an unmatched culinary experience, where street food comes alive and invites your senses on an unforgettable journey.

Go ahead, explore these markets, and treat yourself to the delicious gastronomy that Colombia has to offer in Bogotá. Buen provecho!

title.rendered: Discover the Destinations: Digital Nomad Visas Around the World

excerpt.rendered: What is a digital nomad visa? It's a permit granted to people who work remotely in a foreign country or who run a business they can manage from anywhere in the world. In this guide, I break down everything you need to know — eligibility criteria, required documents, costs, processing times, and a full list of countries offering this visa in 2024.

content.rendered:
What Is a Digital Nomad Visa?

A digital nomad visa is a permit granted to people who work remotely in a foreign country different from their country of origin, or who run a business that can be managed from anywhere in the world.

Basic Eligibility Criteria for a Digital Nomad Visa

To obtain a digital nomad visa, you generally need to meet the following criteria:

  • Be over 18 years of age.
  • Demonstrate a specific monthly income (this requirement varies by country).
  • Have a job that can be performed from anywhere in the world.

How to Apply for a Digital Nomad Visa

The process for applying for a digital nomad visa generally includes the following steps:

  1. Complete the application form online or in person, depending on the country's requirements.
  2. Schedule an appointment with the embassy or consulate, if necessary.
  3. Prepare and submit the required documents, which may include a passport, proof of health insurance, proof of income, and documentation related to your remote work.
  4. Attend a visa interview, if required.
  5. Pay the application fee, which varies by country.

Do I Need to Schedule an Appointment with an Embassy or Consulate?

The need to schedule an appointment with an embassy or consulate varies by country. It's important to check the specific requirements of your destination country for a digital nomad visa.

If that's the case, look for a visa office — which could be an embassy or a consulate; keep in mind these are not the same institution. Depending on your country of residence, you may need to apply at a neighboring embassy. Once you locate your visa office, you'll need to schedule an appointment to submit your application and attend the visa interview.

In countries where you can enter as a tourist without a visa, you can travel first and then begin the process directly at the immigration or foreign affairs office of the country you're in.

Required Documents for a Digital Nomad Visa Application

The documents needed to apply for a digital nomad visa may include:

  • Digital nomad visa application form.
  • Valid passport.
  • Identification photographs.
  • Proof of health insurance.
  • Proof of financial income.
  • Proof of remote work employment.
  • Civil documents such as a birth certificate and CV.

Processing Time and Validity of a Digital Nomad Visa

Processing time and validity vary by country. Generally, the application process takes about a month, and the visa can be valid for one or two years, with the possibility of extending it to five years in some cases.

How Much Does a Digital Nomad Visa Cost?

The cost of a digital nomad visa can vary considerably by country. Fees typically range from $200 to $2,000, depending on the country and the type of visa requested.

Some Caribbean countries such as Barbados and Antigua and Barbuda have the highest application fees for digital nomad visas — up to $3,000 for family applications. However, it's worth carefully reviewing the information, because some countries like Georgia offer digital nomad visas completely free of charge to attract more applicants. There are also variations based on international agreements between countries — for example, for Spanish citizens, the digital nomad visa in Colombia is free.

Digital Nomad Visa Denial

Common reasons a digital nomad visa may be denied include a criminal record, lack of adequate remote work, providing false information on the application form, and failing to meet the income requirements.

Differences Between a Digital Nomad Visa and a Tourist Visa

The main differences between a digital nomad visa and a tourist visa involve work rights, the length of permitted stay, and tax restrictions:

  • Digital Nomad Visa: Takes months to process. Valid for more than 90 days. Allows you to travel and work. Requires employment to obtain.
  • Tourist Visa: Takes a couple of weeks to process. Usually valid for no more than 90 days. Only allows travel, not work. Does not require employment.

Taxes and Work with a Digital Nomad Visa

Generally, you must file a tax return in your country of residence while holding a digital nomad visa. However, this varies depending on the country you're working in. For example, if you hold a digital nomad visa in Estonia and stay for more than 183 days, you are considered a tax resident. On the other hand, some countries offer a one-year tax exemption for digital nomads — check in advance with your host country.

What Kind of Work Can I Do with a Digital Nomad Visa?

Generally, you can do any type of work with a digital nomad visa, as long as it can be performed remotely via the internet. The most popular types of work for digital nomads include:

  • Content creators.
  • Web developers.
  • Social media marketing.
  • Digital entrepreneurs.
  • Virtual assistants.

Countries Offering Digital Nomad Visas in 2024

  • 🇦🇮 Anguilla
  • 🇦🇬 Antigua and Barbuda
  • 🇦🇷 Argentina
  • 🇦🇼 Aruba
  • 🇧🇸 Bahamas
  • 🇧🇧 Barbados
  • 🇧🇿 Belize
  • 🇧🇷 Brazil
  • 🇨🇻 Cape Verde
  • 🇰🇷 South Korea
  • 🇨🇿 Czech Republic
  • 🇨🇾 Cyprus
  • 🇨🇴 Colombia
  • 🇨🇷 Costa Rica
  • 🇭🇷 Croatia
  • 🇨🇼 Curaçao
  • 🇦🇪 Dubai
  • 🇪🇨 Ecuador
  • 🇪🇸 Spain
  • 🇪🇪 Estonia
  • 🇬🇪 Georgia
  • 🇬🇷 Greece
  • 🇭🇺 Hungary
  • 🇮🇩 Indonesia
  • 🇮🇸 Iceland
  • 🇧🇲 Bermuda
  • 🇰🇾 Cayman Islands
  • 🇮🇹 Italy
  • 🇱🇻 Latvia
  • 🇲🇰 North Macedonia
  • 🇲🇹 Malta
  • 🇲🇺 Mauritius
  • 🇲🇽 Mexico
  • 🇲🇪 Montenegro
  • 🇲🇸 Montserrat
  • 🇳🇴 Norway
  • 🇵🇦 Panama
  • 🇵🇹 Portugal
  • 🇩🇴 Dominican Republic
  • 🇷🇴 Romania
  • 🇱🇨 Saint Lucia
  • 🇸🇨 Seychelles
  • 🇱🇰 Sri Lanka
  • 🇿🇦 South Africa
  • 🇹🇭 Thailand
  • 🇹🇼 Taiwan
  • 🇺🇾 Uruguay

title.rendered: Delights of Colombia: A Journey Through Colombian Gastronomy Through My Favorite Dishes

excerpt.rendered: Long before I became a digital nomad, I visited Bogotá on a vacation trip and fell in love with many things about the Colombian capital. But without a doubt, what conquered me most was the food — its bold Latin American flavors, its incredible variety of soups, and the pride Colombians take in their gastronomy. Here are my 8 personal favorites from a first year of living in Bogotá.

content.rendered:
Colombian Flavors and Wonders

Long before I became a digital nomad, I visited Bogotá on a vacation trip and fell in love with many things about the Colombian capital. But without a doubt, what conquered me most was the food — and it did so for several reasons:

  • Because it belongs to that extraordinary Latin American gastronomy, flavorful and crafted from local ingredients.
  • Because of its incredible variety of delicious broths and soups.
  • Because of how proud Colombians are of their gastronomy.

Colombia is a country full of contrasts — famous not only for its beautiful landscapes, but also for its incredibly rich and varied cuisine. From the Caribbean coast to the Andean mountains and vast plains, each region has something special to offer in culinary terms. The combination of fresh ingredients, traditional techniques, and a unique blend of cultures makes Colombian cuisine one of the most diverse and delicious in the world.

Colombia on My Plate: My Personal Picks from Colombian Cuisine

What I've enjoyed most since arriving in Colombia has been the food. It's very similar to that of my homeland — Ecuador: the constant presence of white rice, the wide variety of delicious soups, and the seasoning, which is very alike, so every time I try a new dish I genuinely enjoy it.

After my first ten months living in Bogotá, I've put together a selection that I'll call my favorites. The list could have been much longer, but somehow I had to narrow it down to my top eight.

Important note: This list is based on my culinary experience in Bogotá. I haven't yet traveled to the Colombian coast — neither the Caribbean nor the Pacific. I'm sure that when that happens, I'll need to revise this list... or start a second one with my new favorites.

1. Bandeja Paisa

Originally from the Antioquia region, the bandeja paisa (Antioquian feast platter) is a feast on a plate. This iconic Colombian dish is a generous mix of rice, beans, chicharrón (fried pork belly), ground beef, fried egg, avocado, and fried sweet plantain. It's a meal that satisfies all the senses with its varied flavors and textures.

2. Ajiaco

Ajiaco is a thick, comforting soup typical of the Bogotá savanna region. This Colombian delight is made with chicken, papa criolla (a small yellow native potato), corn on the cob, regular potato, capers, and cream. It's served with white rice, avocado, and hot pepper. It is the perfect dish to warm the soul on a cold day.

3. Chicharrón

Chicharrón (fried pork belly) is a crispy and delicious temptation, popular all over Colombia. It consists of pieces of pork belly — the part that includes the rind, fat, and some meat — fried or roasted until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining juicy and flavorful on the inside. It's commonly served with an arepa (corn cake) and guacamole.

4. Cazuela de Mariscos

From the Caribbean coast of Colombia comes this exquisite preparation. The cazuela de mariscos (seafood casserole) is a blend of octopus, shrimp, squid, and fish cooked in a broth made with coconut cream. It's an explosion of flavor and freshness that will transport you directly to the Colombian beaches.

Personal note: "I urgently need to get to the Colombian coast." 😅

5. Lechona

Lechona is a traditional dish from the Tolima region. It consists of a whole pig stuffed with rice, a pea stew, and spices, slow-cooked in a clay oven. The skin becomes crispy and golden while the meat turns tender and full of flavor. It is an indispensable dish at any Colombian celebration.

Colombian Gastronomy at Breakfast Time

6. Caldo de Costilla

Originally from the Boyacá region, the caldo de costilla (beef rib broth) is a comforting dish, perfect for fighting off the cold of the mountains. Made with beef ribs slow-cooked with potato, corn on the cob, onion, and cilantro, it is a true delight that warms the body and the soul.

7. Pandebono

Pandebono is a cheese bread roll typical of the Valle del Cauca region. Made with yuca starch, fresh cheese, eggs, and sugar, it's fluffy on the inside and slightly crispy on the outside. Served warm, it's the perfect accompaniment to a good Colombian tinto (black coffee).

8. Changua

Originally from the Cundinamarca region, changua is a comforting and delicious breakfast soup. Made with milk, eggs, onion, and cilantro, it is smooth, creamy, and full of flavor. Served with pieces of bread to dip, it's the perfect way to start the day.

Colombian Gastronomy: Conclusion

Colombian cuisine is a sensory experience you cannot miss. From the mountains to the sea, every dish tells a story of tradition, flavor, and passion. The bandeja paisa, ajiaco, chicharrón, cazuela de mariscos, caldo de costilla, lechona, pandebono, and changua are just a small sample of the culinary richness Colombia has to offer.

Go ahead and try these delicious dishes — discover for yourself why Colombian food is so beloved around the world!
